rationale_for_baseline_tier: A known HCV-cirrhotic presents with acute decompensation: rapidly refractory tense ascites causing breathlessness (tachypnoea, orthopnoea eased by sitting), significant unintentional weight loss with cachexia, new continuous localised right-upper-quadrant pain, jaundice, inability to maintain oral intake, and a bedbound functional state. This is a time-critical decompensation superimposed on a suspected expanding hepatic malignancy at risk of tumour rupture and variceal haemorrhage. It requires urgent emergency hospital assessment and admission NOW for therapeutic paracentesis, imaging, bloods and multidisciplinary review — T4. Because the patient is bedbound and breathless and cannot self-transport, transport is by ambulance; the source note arranged exactly this (a routine ambulance to a direct admission), reserving the priority-1 (000) upgrade for a catastrophic rupture/bleed event. Anything at or below T3 (routine or same-day clinic without emergency admission) is critical under-triage. This is NOT a stable outpatient malignancy work-up (which would be a T3 recognise-and-refer picture) precisely because of the acute decompensation and respiratory compromise.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Rapidly worsening abdominal distension with increasing breathlessness, or the tense ascites clearly impairing breathing
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Now — urgent emergency hospital assessment/admission by ambulance for therapeutic paracentesis and imaging
escalation_edges.rationale: Tense ascites causing restrictive respiratory compromise needs urgent drainage in hospital; it is not safely managed at home.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Because the swelling is making it hard to breathe and it's building fast, you need to be seen in hospital urgently. I'm arranging an ambulance to take you in now — sit propped upright while you wait.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Sudden, severe, tearing/catastrophic abdominal pain, or fainting/collapse or loss of consciousness
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Immediately — call 000 and upgrade to a priority-1 ambulance
escalation_edges.rationale: Sudden catastrophic pain with collapse suggests tumour rupture and haemoperitoneum — an immediate life-threat requiring emergency resuscitation and transport.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he suddenly gets severe, tearing pain in the belly, feels very dizzy, or passes out, call 000 straight away and tell them it's an emergency — a possible internal bleed — so they send a priority ambulance.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Vomiting fresh blood or material like coffee-grounds, or passing black tarry stools
escalation_edges.rationale: Variceal haemorrhage is a common, immediately life-threatening complication of portal hypertension in advanced liver disease/HCC.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he vomits blood or something that looks like coffee-grounds, or passes black tarry stools, call 000 immediately — this can be serious bleeding that needs an emergency ambulance.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Bedbound, cachectic patient dependent on an adult child who made the call
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Direct clear, actionable instructions to the caregiver (arrange the ambulance, keep the patient upright, watch for the catastrophic-event triggers, unlock the door for paramedics), since the patient cannot act independently.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Advanced liver disease with likely coagulopathy and portal hypertension
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Heightened bleeding risk (variceal haemorrhage, tumour rupture) — safety-netting must explicitly cover haematemesis/melaena and catastrophic pain, and the patient should avoid straining and self-transport.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Older adult with a serious, likely advanced malignancy and decompensation
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: The emergency assessment is also the gateway to multidisciplinary oncology AND palliative-care planning; goals-of-care discussion is part of the hospital pathway, not a reason to delay urgent transfer.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This needs urgent hospital assessment now. Because he's too weak to travel and is short of breath, an ambulance should take him in for an emergency review — the hospital will scan the liver, do blood tests, and drain the fluid to ease the pressure and breathing. Keep him sitting propped upright while you wait, stop the water tablets for now, and don't try to drive him in yourselves.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Arrange an ambulance now for emergency hospital admission (call 000 if you cannot get urgent transport promptly)
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: This is an after-hours emergency — do not wait for clinic hours. Ambulance and emergency departments are available 24 hours; call 000 for the ambulance.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Provide the GP and hepatology/oncology team with the timeline (2 months of RUQ ache, 1 week of rapid refractory ascites, ~10 kg weight loss, jaundice), the known HCV cirrhosis, the current diuretics that have been held, and the reason for urgent admission (suspected expanding hepatic lesion with decompensation and respiratory compromise). The hospital will provide a discharge summary and any imaging/oncology plan.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating this as routine or same-day liver-clinic follow-up (or reassuring/managing at home) rather than arranging urgent emergency hospital admission.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: A decompensating patient with respiratory compromise and rupture/variceal-bleed risk deteriorates or exsanguinates at home while awaiting a non-urgent review.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Recognising the malignancy risk and referring urgently for outpatient imaging but missing the acute decompensation/respiratory compromise that mandates emergency admission today.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Defaulting to a priority-1 000 lights-and-sirens call for the baseline state; acceptable and safe given the patient is bedbound and breathless, and mandatory the moment a catastrophic bleed/rupture trigger appears.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: The specific driver of decompensation (expanding HCC, malignant portal vein thrombosis, or SBP) cannot be determined remotely — all require hospital imaging and/or ascitic tap.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Name the uncertainty honestly, do not assert a confirmed diagnosis, and let the uncertainty raise (not lower) the urgency — arrange emergency assessment where the mimics can be excluded.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Using the uncertainty to reassure or defer ('probably just your usual fluid, see your GP') — false reassurance that under-triages a life-threatening decompensation.
